# ZH→EN Lyric Translation: Linguistic Difference Rules

Use this file together with `reference/common.md`. Common rules (format protection, workflow, Red Lines, special line handling) are in `common.md`.

**Line length limit (ZH→EN specific):** Target **6–16 syllables** per English line; never exceed **18 syllables**. Split at original timeline breakpoints if needed. Each Chinese character counts as approximately **1 beat**; target a comparable number of English stress beats.

---

## 1. Topic-Prominence and Pro-Drop → Subject Restoration

Chinese is a topic-prominent, pro-drop language. Subjects are frequently omitted once established. English requires explicit subjects in most cases.

| Situation | English Restoration Strategy | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Clear single narrator | Restore `I` | `走不了` → `I can't leave` |
| Narrator addressing someone | Restore `you` | `等太久了` → `you've waited too long` |
| Third-person absent referent | Restore `she` / `he` / `they` | `不见了` → `she's disappeared` |
| Topic-fronted clause | Restructure: topic becomes subject or object | `那个人，忘不了` → `that person I can never forget` |
| Universal/impersonal statement | Use `we` / `it` / omit | `时间过了` → `time has passed` |
| Continuous omission across lines | Build subject map for the section before translating | — |

## 2. Chinese Tense and Aspect Markers → English Tense

Chinese has no inflectional tense; aspect is expressed by particles, adverbs, and context.

| Chinese Marker | Meaning | English Rendering |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `了` (completion) | Action completed | Simple past: "walked", "ended" |
| `了` (state change) | Situation has changed | "now", "at last", perfect: "have become" |
| `着` (ongoing state) | Continuous, stative | Progressive: "is…-ing" / "was…-ing" |
| `过` (experience) | Has happened before | "have…before" / "once" |
| `正在` / `在` | Right now | Present progressive |
| `将` / `会` (future) | Will happen | "will" / "going to" |
| `曾经` | Once, in the past | "used to" / "once I…" |

## 3. Chengyu and Classical Allusions → Meaning-Based Translation

Chengyu (成语) are 4-character idioms with deep cultural meaning. Never translate the individual characters literally.

| Chinese Allusion | Literal Characters | Core Meaning | English Rendering |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `海阔天空` | sea wide sky empty | Boundless freedom | "boundless" / "free as the open sky" |
| `以刚克刚` | use hard overcome hard | Fight fire with fire | "fight fire with fire" |
| `不羁放纵` | not restrain indulge | Wild and free | "wild and free" |
| `若有所失` | like something lost | A hollow ache of loss | "something missing" / "a hollow in my chest" |
| `沧海` | vast blue ocean | Immensity of the sea (not just literal ocean) | "vast ocean" / "endless sea" / "this great unknown" |
| `消失在人海` | disappear in people-sea | Vanish into the crowd | "lost in the crowd" / "gone into the masses" |

> **Rule**: If you are uncertain about a chengyu's meaning, describe the core emotional concept in English rather than rendering the characters.

## 4. Chinese Parallel Structure (排比/对偶) → English Parallel Phrasing

Chinese lyric poetry loves parallel structures: 4-4-4-4, 3-3-3-3, or 7-7-7-7 character groups.

- **Maintain syntactic parallelism in English**: if Chinese uses three verb phrases, use three English verb phrases of similar length.
- **Match stress beats, not syllable count**: a 4-character group → approx. 2–3 English stress beats.
- **Rhythm first**: prefer parallel English phrases that scan naturally over accurate but uneven phrasing.

> **Example**:  
> Chinese: `你来了 又走了 / 你说了 不说了`  
> Wrong: `You arrived and you departed / You said something and then you didn't say anything`  
> Correct: `you came, then left / you spoke, then went silent`

## 5. Chinese Modal Adverbs → Precise English Equivalents

These modal adverbs carry emotional intensity and must not be flattened to generic English.

| Chinese Adverb | Core Emotional Meaning | English Equivalent |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `偏偏` | "of all things" / ironically, exactly when I didn't want this | "of all things" / "just my luck" / "somehow" |
| `明明` | "clearly" / the speaker knows something painfully well | "clearly" / "obviously" / "I know perfectly well" |
| `终究` | "in the end" / inevitable conclusion | "in the end" / "at last" / "still" |
| `好想` | "I so want to" / intense longing | "I so want to" / "desperately want to" / "all I want is" |
| `只是` | "but" / "just" / subtle concession | "but" / "just" / "only" |
| `总算` | "finally" after long waiting/effort | "finally" / "at long last" |
| `不知不觉` | without realizing, time slipped away | "without realizing" / "before I knew it" |
| `好不容易` | barely managed to, after great effort | "barely managed to" / "after so long" |
| `竟然` | to my surprise, unexpectedly | "somehow" / "to my surprise" / "of all things" |

## 6. Reduplication (叠词/叠字) → English Emphasis

Chinese reduplication expresses gradual, gentle, or intensified action.

| Chinese Reduplication | Feeling | English Rendering |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `慢慢` | slowly, gradually | "slowly" / "slowly slowly" |
| `轻轻` | gently, softly | "gently" / "softly" |
| `悄悄` | quietly, secretly | "quietly" / "in secret" |
| `深深` | deeply, profoundly | "deeply" / "so deep" |
| `久久` | for a long long time | "for so long" / "endlessly" |
| `远远` | from far away | "from afar" / "far in the distance" |
| `静静` | quietly, still | "in silence" / "quietly still" |

## 7. Genre and Register Mapping

| Chinese Genre | Typical Vocal Style | English Register Strategy |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 现代流行情歌 (Mandopop ballad) | Conversational, intimate | Natural contractions, accessible vocabulary |
| 粤语港台摇滚 (Cantopop/rock) | Anthemic, bold | Shorter punchy lines, strong verbs, declarative |
| 古风/国风 (Classical-style) | Poetic, elevated | Elevated diction, nature imagery, archaic phrasing if consistent |
| 说唱 (Rap) | Fast, colloquial, punchy | Street slang acceptable, clipped sentences |
| 励志 (Inspirational) | Soaring, declarative | Strong future tense, "we will", "rise", "stand" |
| 粤语老歌 (Classic Canto) | Lush, melodic | Lyrical, romantic; more syllables tolerated |

## 8. Chinese Negation → English Negation

| Chinese Negation | Specific Tone | English Equivalent |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `不` | Won't / don't (habitual, intentional) | "won't" / "don't" |
| `没` / `没有` | Didn't / hasn't (factual past) | "didn't" / "haven't" |
| `别` | Don't (imperative) | "don't" / "please don't" |
| `甭` | No need to (colloquial) | "no need to" / "don't bother" |
| `绝不` | Absolutely will not | "never" / "I absolutely won't" |
| `从没有` | Never has | "never have" / "not once" |
| `哪会` | How could I possibly | "how could I" / "I could never" |

## 9. Common Error Types (ZH→EN)

**Error 1: Translating 成语 character by character.**
- Source: `海阔天空`
- Wrong: `sea-wide sky-empty`
- Correct: `free as the open sky`

**Error 2: Dropping modal adverb emotional precision.**
- Source: `偏偏是你`
- Wrong: `it's you`
- Correct: `of all people, it had to be you`

**Error 3: Losing parallel structure.**
- Source: `你来了 又走了`
- Wrong: `You came to me but then you turned around and walked away`
- Correct: `you came, then left`

**Error 4: Not restoring a pro-dropped subject.**
- Source: `走不了啊`
- Wrong: `Can't leave`
- Correct: `I can't leave`

**Error 5: Translating `了` as "already" every time.**
- Source: `下雨了` in lyric context
- Wrong: `it already rained`
- Correct: `the rain has come` / `it's raining now`

**Error 6: Ignoring tone of reduplication.**
- Source: `轻轻的` (lyric context: tender farewell)
- Wrong: `lightly`
- Correct: `so softly` / `gently`

## 10. Final Checklist (7 Points)

- [ ] **Subject restored**: Every line has an appropriate explicit subject; no ghost subjects.
- [ ] **Aspect markers**: 了/着/过/曾经/正在 correctly rendered as English tense/aspect, not defaulted to past.
- [ ] **成语/allusions**: No character-by-character translation; meaning extracted.
- [ ] **Parallel structure**: Chinese 排比/对偶 maintained with syntactically parallel English phrases.
- [ ] **Modal adverbs**: 偏偏/明明/终究/好想 etc. rendered with full emotional precision.
- [ ] **Line length**: No line exceeds 18 syllables.
- [ ] **No plot additions**: No characters, settings, or emotions introduced beyond what the source contains.
